title: Notice of termination and final pay

meta.description: Minimum notice periods for termination and what must be included in an employee's final pay.

headings.text: Notice of termination and final pay
Who is not entitled to notice?
What must be in the final pay?
When must final pay be paid?

content:
When an employer ends an employee's employment (dismissal), they are generally required to give the employee notice of termination, unless the employee is being dismissed for serious misconduct.

Minimum notice periods (Fair Work Act 2009, s.117):
Notice periods are based on the employee's period of continuous service:

- Up to 1 year: 1 week
- More than 1 year, up to 3 years: 2 weeks
- More than 3 years, up to 5 years: 3 weeks
- More than 5 years: 4 weeks

Additional notice for employees aged 45+:
If an employee is over 45 years of age and has completed at least 2 years of continuous service, they are entitled to an extra 1 week of notice on top of the above.

Who is not entitled to minimum notice?
The following employees are generally NOT entitled to minimum notice under the NES:
- Casual employees
- Employees engaged for a specified period or task (fixed-term/task contracts)
- Employees dismissed for serious misconduct
- Seasonal employees
- Trainees (if the contract ends at the completion of training)

Note: Awards or enterprise agreements may provide additional notice entitlements.

Payment in lieu of notice:
An employer can pay the employee their full wages for the notice period instead of requiring them to work the notice period. This is called payment in lieu of notice and must equal the amount the employee would have been paid if they had worked throughout the notice period.

Employee resignation notice:
When an employee resigns, they may also be required to give notice. The minimum notice they must give is typically 1 week (or as specified in their award/agreement/contract). If an employee doesn't give sufficient notice, the employer may be able to withhold money from final pay (subject to award provisions).

What must be in the final pay?
An employee's final pay must include:
1. Outstanding wages for all hours worked (including any overtime/penalty rates)
2. Any accrued annual leave (paid out at the applicable rate, including leave loading if the employee normally receives it)
3. Long service leave entitlement (if applicable — state/territory laws apply)
4. Any other accrued entitlements (e.g. rostered days off, time off in lieu)
5. Payment in lieu of notice (if applicable)
6. Redundancy pay (if applicable — see redundancy page)

Note: Unused sick/carer's leave is NOT paid out on termination.

When must final pay be paid?
Final pay must generally be paid on the last day of employment or as soon as practicable. Some awards specify a timeframe. Failure to pay on time may constitute a breach of the Fair Work Act.
